title: Teaching & Service
text:
I231 Mathematics of Cybersecurity / Introduction to Cryptography
Lead Associate Instructor, Spring 2023
Associate Instructor, Spring 2022, Spring 2026
This course introduces students to mathematical and programming tools used in modern security research and practice, including functional programming, probability theory, analysis of algorithms, complexity theory, number theory, and group theory.
As Lead Associate Instructor, I managed teaching assistants, designed assignments, prepared homework solutions, held office hours, graded homework and exams, and occasionally taught class sessions.
As Associate Instructor, I graded homework, quizzes, and exams; held office hours; and prepared homework solutions.
I535 Economics of Information Security
Lead Associate Instructor, Fall 2024
Associate Instructor, Fall 2022
This graduate seminar examines economic measures of cybercrime, interactions among actors and institutions involved in cybercrime, risk perception, usability, privacy, and rational evaluation of risk in e-crime contexts. I supported students in navigating current research and theoretical perspectives on cybercrime and information security economics.
As Lead Associate Instructor, I managed teaching assistants, designed assignments, prepared solutions, held office hours, graded homework and exams, and occasionally manages class discussion sessions.
As Associate Instructor, I graded assignments and exams, held office hours, and supported student learning.
I495 Design and Development of Information Systems
Associate Instructor, Fall 2020 and Spring 2021
This capstone course guides senior students through the full cycle of system development in teams, including project scoping, user evaluation, front-end and back-end development, testing, and presentation of a working system.
As Associate Instructor,I met weekly with student groups, tracked project progress, consulted on development challenges, and helped teams translate start-up-style ideas into feasible and usable information systems. One of the teams I mentored, Arts at IU, received the Top Project Recognition Award in 2021.





Fundamentals of Information Systems
Teaching Assistant, Fall 2018 and Spring 2019
Supported instruction, grading, and student learning in an introductory information systems course.

title: Dalya Manatova
text:
Google Scholar  GitHub  LinkedIn  X/Twitter RG ResearchGate 

I study how adversarial, outlawed, and socio-technical communities organize, coordinate, persist, and adapt over time. My research combines network science, graph learning, discourse analysis, and organizational theory to model cybercrime ecosystems, ransomware operations, underground communities, and open-source software coordination.
I am especially interested in resilience, interdependence, specialization, and supply-chain structures in these communities.

 CV  Publications

title: Dalya Manatova
text:
Current Projects

Temporal Graphs of Open-Source Software Patch Review Workflows
Modeling patch review workflows, contributor/maintainer coordination and socio-technical dependencies in Linux kernel development.


Community Hiding from Graph Learning-Based Clustering
Studying how groups can evade or resist GNN-based community detection in attributed networks, with implications for group privacy, surveillance, and adversarial adaptation.


Ransomware Operations and Supply-Chain Simulation
Developing simulation models of ransomware operations to better understand cybercrime supply chains, task interdependencies, disruption, and operational recovery.


Organizational Anatomy of Cybercrime
Developing multilayer models of cybercrime organizational resilience by analyzing structure, specialization, rules and norms, shared goals, and relationship dynamics.
